theme: Why a Berthing Aid System (BAS) is more than a pretty mimic panel
hook: When the quay and the bridge argue, DockMoor is the shared picture worth paying for.
technical_angle: DockMoor visualises approach and mooring in real time—distance, speed, angle—so corrective action is data-led, not anecdote-led.

theme: Dockmoor-BM: bow/stern speed and angle against the fender line
hook: Berthing is geometry with consequences; a scale image beats a gut feeling in squall season.
technical_angle: BM measures ship–berth geometry to build a live manoeuvre view: alerts, history, and replay for incident learning.

theme: Dockmoor-MS: mooring stress where QRH load cells actually live
hook: Mooring lines do not fail in the spreadsheet; they fail at the hook.
technical_angle: MS tracks rope tension, hook status, fender compression—often via load cells integrated in QRH bolts—with overload alarms operators can trust.

theme: Dockmoor-HDT: hardware diagnostics over UDP—no poetry, just state
hook: If a device is silent on the network, it is still telling you something—usually the wrong story.
technical_angle: HDT communicates via UDP to surface panels, lasers, load cells, loading arms, and weather kits—one diagnostic layer for the whole manoeuvre chain.

theme: Dockmoor-ER: when the berth needs weather and sea state in the same UI
hook: Wind shifts and swell periods are part of the berthing equation, not footnotes.
technical_angle: ER ties compact stations to wind, waves, humidity, pressure—and can extend to visibility or radiation where the facility demands it.

theme: Dockmoor-FP: fender deflection in the last approach metres
hook: A fender that looks ‘fine’ can still be storing asymmetric energy.
technical_angle: FP archives deflection, triggers audio/visual alarms on excessive deformation, and keeps history for claims and design feedback.

theme: Dockmoor-LD: loading-arm drift monitoring during mooring
hook: Hydrocarbon arms do not forgive casual geometry.
technical_angle: LD tracks arm drift in real time with configurable alarms—measurements stay available for later analysis and reporting.

theme: Dockmoor-RR: local, remote, and emergency firing philosophy for QRH
hook: Release is a safety function; convenience must never outrank procedure.
technical_angle: RR combines interactive monitoring of line tension and hook status with control desks for local/remote release—including emergency sequences.

theme: Dockmoor-WEB: BAS in the browser without inventing a new VPN horror story
hook: If the superintendent needs the manoeuvre on a tablet, ship it securely—not ‘just RDP to someone’s laptop’.
technical_angle: WEB serves DockMoor over LAN or Internet to PCs, tablets, and phones—access becomes an ops decision, not an IT science project.

theme: From alarms to evidence: reports, replay, and past manoeuvres in DockMoor
hook: Auditors love screenshots; engineers love time-synchronized replay.
technical_angle: BAS registers events and supports playback—turning ‘what happened?’ into a traceable narrative for insurers and internal learning.

theme: Quick release hooks: why integrated load paths beat ‘smart rope’ theatre
hook: Mooring is mechanical first; telemetry is there to protect people and steel.
technical_angle: Prosertek QRH assemblies anchor mooring lines with remote/local release options and sensible paths for tension sensing—paired with Dockmoor-RR when automation is in scope.

theme: QRH coatings: C5-M ISO 12944-6 and NORSOK M-501 in real project language
hook: Salt fog does not read datasheets; it reads welds and edges.
technical_angle: Epoxy systems target abrasion, seawater, oils, and hydrocarbon spills—with C5-M / NORSOK-class options when the environment demands it.

theme: Materials honesty: cast steel hooks, rolled bases, heat-treated shafts
hook: A QRH is not a single ‘part’; it is a stack of metallurgical decisions.
technical_angle: Prosertek publishes material splits—cast bodies/teeth, rolled structural sections, machined and heat-treated shafts—so FAT/SAT debates stay factual.

theme: Capstans on QRH: pedal-activated, oil-filled reducers, hands-free line handling
hook: Hands belong on guardrails, not wrestling lines at the wrong moment.
technical_angle: Integrated capstans favour reversible rotation, stretched drums for fairlead, and pedal control—often bundled with hook assemblies in live projects.

theme: Double, triple, quadruple QRH configurations: matching tonnage to reality
hook: Catalogues sell ‘units’; berths sell ‘load cases’.
technical_angle: Prosertek supplies multi-hook assemblies (e.g., 75–150 T-class references on major LNG and product terminals) sized to client mooring plans.

theme: Marine gangways: telescopic bridges for dock-to-ship under harsh conditions
hook: Passenger patience is finite; mechanical margin is not optional.
technical_angle: Tower or column configurations extend Prosertek gangways across air gaps with designs aimed at tough environments and repeatable operations.

theme: Boarding bridges: weather protection as part of the transit experience
hook: If people are exposed, operations slow down—then risk compounds.
technical_angle: Boarding bridges target adaptable transit between ship and shore under severe conditions, with layouts that suit different vessel interfaces.

theme: Port cranes on gangways and towers: load paths the whole system must respect
hook: A crane is never ‘just’ an accessory; it is a dynamic load on a walking path.
technical_angle: Prosertek positions cranes as integrated harbour equipment—range, structure, and accessories depend on the gangway/tower concept.

theme: Fender systems: absorbing berthing energy without pretending physics is negotiable
hook: Good fender selection is energy accounting, not brand preference.
technical_angle: Prosertek supplies docking fender systems to protect vessels and structures—Dockmoor-FP closes the loop with deflection monitoring during operations.

theme: Bollards as mooring anchors: when ‘old iron’ needs an honest conversation
hook: A bollard is not immortal; concrete and anchors age invisibly.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s bollard portfolio underpins conventional mooring; pairing design knowledge with field testing culture keeps assumptions honest.

theme: Non-destructive bollard testing: vibration, sensors, and invisible cracks
hook: You cannot manage what you pretend is still ‘probably fine’.
technical_angle: Prosertek performs NDT on installed bollards—wave-based excitation and sensors—to reveal cracks, wear, and foundation issues before they become headlines.

theme: BollardScan + Lloyd’s Register: third-party credibility for mooring assurance
hook: A method approved by class is a shared language with insurers and authorities.
technical_angle: Prosertek works with BollardScan’s long maritime track record; verification aligns with Lloyd’s Register expectations and yields detailed reporting.

theme: Recommended workload and 3-year certificate narrative: turning scans into policy
hook: A number without a maintenance story is just a moment in time.
technical_angle: NDT packages aim at recommended workloads, monitoring guidance, and a certificate package that supports wharf governance—not just a PDF trophy.

theme: Underwater maintenance as part of harbour civil health
hook: What you cannot see still loads your mooring plan.
technical_angle: Prosertek lists underwater maintenance among services—complementing topside automation with substructure reality.

theme: Portable Pilot Unit (PPU): piloting assistance as a Prosertek service line
hook: Pilots bridge the gap between chart and quay; tools should bridge pilot and port data.
technical_angle: PPU appears as a dedicated offering alongside hardware—worth pairing with BAS narratives for shared situational awareness.

theme: Engineering and R+D+i: why turnkey is a systems problem, not a SKU list
hook: Clients rarely buy ‘a hook’; they buy a verified mooring story.
technical_angle: Prosertek highlights 20+ years of maritime equipment experience, internal R+D+i, and turnkey delivery from design through installation—coordination beats cost drift from role fragmentation.

theme: Technical Assistance Service (SAT): keeping DockMoor and hooks operationally honest
hook: Software without support ages into folklore; mechanics without records age into incidents.
technical_angle: SAT is the long arc after commissioning—aligned with Prosertek’s global technical assistance positioning on product pages.

theme: Spanish Atlantic and Mediterranean references: BAS modules in real energy ports
hook: If your system survives Cartagena weather and LNG cadence, it has stories worth telling.
technical_angle: Public references cite combinations of Dockmoor-BM/MS/ER/HDT/FP/LD/RR/WEB across major Spanish ports and energy clients—use them as evidence, not vibes.

theme: LNG and oil & gas terminals: where BAS modules stack for risk reduction
hook: Energy berths punish both hurry and hesitation—visibility must be simultaneous.
technical_angle: Prosertek positions BAS across LNG and O&G sectors; module stacks reflect jetty complexity (environment, mooring stress, arms, hooks).

theme: Commercial vs tourist ports: same physics, different operational tempo
hook: A ferry berth and an LNG jetty share equations; they do not share patience.
technical_angle: BAS configurability per port type is the product point—DockMoor modules scale from compact marinas to large commercial harbours.

theme: Brochures that match the engineering: BAS catalog and hooks brochure
hook: Marketing should be a compressed FAT, not a fairy tale.
technical_angle: Prosertek offers downloadable BAS and QRH brochures—use them as the canonical vocabulary for client conversations.

theme: New technologies in port equipment: the guide download as a learning spine
hook: If ‘innovation’ is not tied to mooring physics, it is just slide filler.
technical_angle: Prosertek promotes a guide on new technologies in port equipment—pair it with DockMoor module talk to keep hype grounded.

theme: Integrating AIS context with DockMoor—without confusing display with control
hook: Awareness layers should converge; authority boundaries should remain boringly clear.
technical_angle: Field discussions often blend AIS/DGPS ship data with BAS—Prosertek’s value is measured manoeuvre + mooring telemetry, not replacing COLREG thinking.

theme: PLC interlocks around QRH release: permissives that respect hot work and weather holds
hook: Automation should refuse unsafe releases without refusing the operator’s agency to document why.
technical_angle: Dockmoor-RR sits in real projects alongside local safety culture—tie release logic to maintenance windows, ESD states, and training drills.

theme: Alarm rationalisation when BM, MS, FP, and ER all talk at once
hook: Alarm floods are a UX failure as much as a sensor failure.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s modular BAS invites prioritisation layers—suppress noise, escalate physics-backed risk, and log operator acknowledgements.

theme: Environmental limits in Dockmoor-ER: from comfort to operational go/no-go
hook: A pretty weather widget is useless if limits are not contractually and operationally defined.
technical_angle: ER supports wind, waves, humidity, pressure—and optional visibility—so limits can be tied to pilotage, loading arms, or gangway rules.

theme: Fender energy and Dockmoor-FP: correlating deflection with approach speed
hook: If BM says the approach is hot, FP should tell you where energy went.
technical_angle: Cross-module narratives turn berthing into a closed-loop story—useful for claims, training, and design feedback.

theme: Loading arm drift (LD) vs mooring line tension (MS): coupling stories
hook: Hydrocarbon hardware and mooring hardware share time but not always causality.
technical_angle: LD+MS together help separate ship motion effects from arm control issues—critical on product and LNG jetties.

theme: Cyber-physical access control for Dockmoor-WEB: LAN vs Internet exposure
hook: Remote visibility is powerful; remote misconfiguration is a category error.
technical_angle: Treat WEB access like OT remote ops: segmentation, MFA, session logging, and least privilege—Prosertek supplies the application; the port supplies the governance.

theme: Data registers and manoeuvre replay: turning BAS into an operational learning system
hook: Replay is training material and dispute material—handle retention policy accordingly.
technical_angle: Prosertek emphasises registers and reproduction of manoeuvres—pair with internal data governance for GDPR and port security rules.

theme: Hook tooth, retention lever, and structural base: vocabulary for maintenance crews
hook: Words matter when you order spares at 02:00.
technical_angle: QRH pages split cast steel bodies/teeth and levers from rolled bases—maintenance plans should mirror that taxonomy.

theme: Epoxy wetting and impact resistance: what ‘marine grade’ actually argues about
hook: Coatings fail at edges and repairs—plan inspections there first.
technical_angle: Prosertek details epoxy behaviour against abrasion, seawater, oils, and spills—match inspection checklists to those failure modes.

theme: Gangway tower vs column: footprint, stiffness, and integration with cranes
hook: Every gangway concept is a compromise between reach, stiffness, and capex.
technical_angle: Prosertek offers tower/column configurations—selection should start from vessel interface and environmental design cases.

theme: Boarding bridges and passenger safety: interlocks that respect weather holds
hook: People will push ‘go’—design the system to make ‘not yet’ understandable.
technical_angle: Pair boarding bridge controls with ER limits and clear HMI narratives—Prosertek’s product framing emphasises severe conditions and adaptability.

theme: Fender selection dialogue: energy absorption vs quay reaction force
hook: Rubber compounds are not interchangeable once berthing energy is honest.
technical_angle: Prosertek supplies fender systems engineered for energy management—tie selection to ship sizes, approach angles, and BM/FP feedback loops.

theme: Bollard pull and NDT: when static capacity is unknowable without a test campaign
hook: Assume less; measure more—especially where concrete age is measured in decades.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s NDT methodology targets anchors, concrete, and steel state—use results to reconcile mooring plans with actual capacity.

theme: Worldwide references: Dunkerque, Genova, Taboguilla alongside Spanish hubs
hook: Global equipment stories beat ‘we are unique’ claims—show receipts.
technical_angle: Prosertek publishes reference lists across Europe and the Americas—use them to anchor sales conversations in evidence.

theme: Energy clients on the record: Enagás, Repsol, Petronor, BP-class narratives
hook: If your hook list matches a major jetty’s reality, say it with humility and specificity.
technical_angle: Public QRH reference tables include major energy terminals—translate tonnage and hook counts into maintenance and BAS module planning.

theme: DockMoor module mix for complex jetties: when clients stack BM through WEB
hook: Complexity is not ‘more screens’—it is more variables under one reproducible timeline.
technical_angle: References show multi-module deployments (e.g., BM+MS+HDT+ER+FP+LD+RR+WEB)—that is systems integration, not a single sensor sale.

theme: Operator training: turning DockMoor replay into competency evidence
hook: Training records should be as inspectable as torque sheets.
technical_angle: Use manoeuvre history and alarm timelines as drill inputs—Prosertek’s BAS story is partly ‘human in the loop with better instruments’.

theme: Insurance and claims: structured DockMoor exports vs screenshots in email
hook: Evidence density wins disputes; opinions do not.
technical_angle: Position BAS registers and module archives as part of operational risk management—not only operations.

theme: Integration workshops: port authority, construction firm, OEM—Prosertek’s stated triangle
hook: Turnkey fails when interfaces are ‘someone else’s problem’.
technical_angle: Engineering pages stress collaboration with authorities, constructors, and engineering firms—run workshops with explicit RACI and interface tables.

theme: Customization vs product catalog discipline
hook: Ports are bespoke; engineering should still be traceable.
technical_angle: Prosertek sells configurable BAS modules and customised mechanical packages—document deviations as controlled changes, not one-off folklore.

theme: Salt, UV, and cyclic loading: why Prosertek emphasises long-lasting designs
hook: Harbour equipment is fatigue and corrosion with a schedule.
technical_angle: The homepage promise—solid, long-lasting designs—should show up in inspection cadence, coating touch-ups, and NDT intervals.

theme: From brochure PDF to commissioning SAT: closing the vocabulary gap
hook: Sales promises and FAT scripts should share nouns.
technical_angle: Use Prosertek brochures as glossary sources for test cases—module names, limits, and interfaces should match.

theme: Prosertek USA and DockMoor: aligning brand entities in client conversations
hook: Legal entities matter for contracts; users care about support continuity.
technical_angle: Site copy references Prosertek USA for DockMoor software—be precise in proposals about supply, service, and spares.

theme: QRH emergency release: choreography between Dockmoor-RR and people on the quay
hook: Emergency paths must be drill-simple; normal paths can be rich.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s RR narrative includes general emergency firing—tie drills to communications, camera coverage, and manual overrides.

theme: Mooring line monitoring without pretending it replaces deck judgement
hook: Telemetry supports decisions; it should not infantilise crew.
technical_angle: Dockmoor-MS is an instrument layer—pair readings with clear procedures for line tending and communications with ship.

theme: Berthing approach limits: translating BM parameters into pilot and bridge language
hook: Different roles use different words for the same metres per second.
technical_angle: Prosertek BM supplies speed/angle context—build shared limit tables for pilotage, tug plans, and BAS alarms.

theme: DockMoor in small ports: proportionate modules vs enterprise overkill
hook: Not every marina needs every module—credibility is fit-for-purpose.
technical_angle: Prosertek lists small ports among sectors—sell the smallest defensible module stack, then expand with evidence.

theme: Logistics and shipbuilding sectors: BAS as a cross-industry mooring monitor
hook: Mooring stress stories extend beyond commercial quays.
technical_angle: Prosertek mentions logistics firms and shipbuilding—use DockMoor where manoeuvre monitoring adds safety or throughput.

theme: Hydrocarbon spill scenarios and QRH coatings: matching material specs to real risk
hook: A coating spec is a scenario statement.
technical_angle: Prosertek lists resistance to oils and petrol-like spills—tie inspection to realistic leak scenarios, not generic checklists.

theme: Concrete cancer near old bollards: NDT as civil diagnostics, not ‘magic scans’
hook: Interpretation matters as much as acquisition.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s NDT narrative includes foundation and surface assessment—pair vendor reports with structural follow-up.

theme: Service contracts: what ‘technical assistance’ should include for DockMoor and hooks
hook: A phone number is not a service; response metrics are.
technical_angle: Define SLAs for software updates, calibration support for load paths, and field visits—aligned with Prosertek’s services positioning.

theme: Spare parts and hooks: traceability from castings to installed fleet
hook: Fleet thinking beats one-off hero repairs.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s reference-heavy QRH lists invite fleet normalization—standardize hook families where possible to simplify spares.

theme: Underwater works and topside automation: one risk register
hook: A clever BAS cannot compensate for a compromised dolphin foundation.
technical_angle: Pair underwater maintenance with bollard NDT and fender inspections—Prosertek offers both lines explicitly.

theme: Portable Pilot Unit in the sales story: bridging navigation and berth execution
hook: PPU complements pilotage; DockMoor complements berth execution—together they reduce translation errors.
technical_angle: Prosertek lists PPU as a service—use it in proposals where pilotage data should meet jetty monitoring.

theme: R+D+i department: translating research into shippable harbour equipment
hook: Innovation is not a slide; it is a drawing revision with a test plan.
technical_angle: Prosertek highlights internal R+D+i—connect it to module roadmap (WEB security, sensor fusion, diagnostics) with evidence.

theme: Turnkey contracting: eliminating cost hikes from fragmented roles
hook: Every hand-off is a budget line item waiting to argue.
technical_angle: Prosertek markets turnkey as coordinated management—proposal structure should show single-threaded accountability.

theme: Client evidence: Petronor-class module stacks as architecture references
hook: Named references are design precedents, not name dropping.
technical_angle: Use published client/module combinations to justify integrated BAS architectures in new projects.

theme: DockMoor and class society narratives: Lloyd’s on bollard testing as a pattern
hook: What gets class-approved becomes easier to insure.
technical_angle: Position NDT and structured reporting as part of a broader assurance story alongside equipment supply.

theme: Berthing damage reduction: the economic argument Prosertek makes explicit
hook: Safety and cost reduction belong in the same paragraph.
technical_angle: Prosertek claims BAS can reduce operational costs and damage to port, fenders, and ships—build simple before/after narratives with conservative assumptions.

theme: Real-time monitoring vs after-the-fact investigations
hook: Investigations are expensive; prevention is engineering.
technical_angle: BAS emphasis on registers and monitoring supports proactive operations—sell it as fewer surprises, not only compliance.

theme: Configuration flexibility: same DockMoor spine, different port personalities
hook: Modules are LEGO with physics constraints.
technical_angle: Prosertek stresses flexible configuration—capture each port’s module rationale in the commissioning record.

theme: Audio/visual alarms on FP and LD: designing for night operations
hook: Alarms must win against wind noise and fatigue.
technical_angle: Prosertek modules reference audio/visual alarms—tune with human factors testing, not only default HMI templates.

theme: UDP in HDT: network design implications for firewalls and diagnostics
hook: Diagnostics love broadcast-friendly protocols; security loves deny-by-default.
technical_angle: Document UDP flows for HDT explicitly in OT segmentation designs—Prosertek names UDP; your network design must name zones.

theme: Laser and oceanographic sensors in HDT: calibration and obsolescence
hook: A laser rangefinder is a recurring calibration relationship.
technical_angle: Prosertek lists lasers and oceanographic sensors under HDT—plan periodic verification and spares.

theme: Weather stations vs compact environmental kits in Dockmoor-ER
hook: Complex stations are powerful when maintenance matches ambition.
technical_angle: Prosertek allows compact to complex—match sensor count to maintenance capacity.

theme: Visibility and radiation add-ons: niche but decisive for some terminals
hook: Some limits are human; some are optical.
technical_angle: Prosertek mentions visibility and solar radiation as optional—use where operations or safety cases require them.

theme: Dockmoor-WEB on smartphones: usability and authentication realities
hook: Mobile access is convenient until it becomes credential sprawl.
technical_angle: Prosertek enables phone/tablet access—enforce device policies and MFA for remote paths.

theme: Comparing DockMoor to ‘generic SCADA screens’: integrated manoeuvre narrative
hook: SCADA without manoeuvre context is just tags with anxiety.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s BAS story is integrated visualization—position against bolt-on dashboards that lack BM/MS/FP coherence.

theme: Hook status and line tension on one RR desk: operator ergonomics
hook: If the desk is confusing, training cannot fix it forever.
technical_angle: Prosertek RR includes interactive monitoring of tension and hook status—validate UI flows with operators, not only with engineers.

theme: General emergency firing: testing without courting chaos
hook: Emergency features deserve the most boring test reports.
technical_angle: Prosertek references general emergency firing—write explicit test scripts and witness records.

theme: Mooring line collection ergonomics: QRH value in commercial terminals
hook: Efficiency matters because time on the berth is money and risk.
technical_angle: Prosertek highlights easier line collection—translate to operational metrics where possible.

theme: Prosertek’s harbour equipment catalog: fenders, cranes, gangways as one ecosystem
hook: Customers buy systems; maintainers live with interfaces.
technical_angle: Homepage groups BAS, QRH, gangways, boarding bridges, cranes, fenders, bollards—proposal narratives should mirror that completeness.

theme: Contact and quote flows: engineering intake done seriously
hook: A quote request should read like a mini engineering brief.
technical_angle: Prosertek pushes request-a-quote—train sales engineers to ask for limits, modules, and interface ownership early.

theme: Downloadable guides: using Prosertek PDFs as training anchors
hook: If the team has not read the brochure, the FAT will be improvisational.
technical_angle: General catalog + technology guide + module brochures—make them required reading before factory tests.

theme: Language coverage: English / Spanish / French sites and global clients
hook: Technical accuracy must survive translation.
technical_angle: Prosertek runs multilingual sites—ensure spec sheets and alarm texts stay consistent across languages in multinational projects.

theme: Prosertek since 1990: longevity as a warranty of institutional memory
hook: Time in market is not automatic expertise—but it is not irrelevant either.
technical_angle: Use company history carefully: cite processes (R+D+i, SAT, references) rather than slogans.

theme: Closing the loop: from DockMoor data to safer mooring culture
hook: Data should make humility easier, not arrogance cheaper.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s BAS promise is safer, more effective operations—measure success in near-miss reduction and repeatable manoeuvres, not only uptime.

theme: Field engineer mindset: representing Prosertek hardware without overselling certainty
hook: The jetty always has one variable you did not model.
technical_angle: Ground public LinkedIn storytelling in Prosertek’s real modules and services—BAS, QRH, gangways, NDT, engineering—avoid fictional products.

theme: Linking www.prosertek.com resources to client workshops
hook: A website should be a shared reference, not a secret.
technical_angle: Use downloadable brochures and service pages as agenda anchors for design reviews—Prosertek’s site is structured for that.

theme: Prosertek equipment and class discussions: evidence bundles
hook: Class and insurers like traceable artifacts.

theme: Future-facing: where DockMoor could absorb new sensor classes responsibly
hook: New sensors are easy; validated limits are hard.
technical_angle: Prosertek’s modular BAS philosophy sets expectations—pilot new sensing under HDT with explicit uncertainty and rollback.

hook: Credibility is also what you do not promise.

hook: Walk the jetty with module names—BM, MS, FP, RR—and you will see integration opportunities faster.

theme: Prosertek in one sentence: mooring and berthing equipment with monitoring that earns trust
hook: If the sentence needs acronyms to sound impressive, rewrite it.
